title: Hot and Sour Soup

seo.imageAlt: Bowl of authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup with mushrooms, tofu, and bamboo shoots.
seo.imageCaption: Warm up with a comforting bowl of homemade Hot and Sour Soup, bursting with traditional Chinese flavors.

description: Hot and Sour Soup is a beloved Chinese classic, celebrated for its perfect harmony of spicy heat and tangy vinegar. This comforting soup features a rich, savory broth loaded with tender shredded pork, silken tofu, earthy mushrooms, and crisp bamboo shoots. It is a wonderfully satisfying appetizer or light meal that truly warms the soul.

ingredients.notes: mixed with 4 tbsp water to make a slurry

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in hot water for 20-30 minutes. Once soft, squeeze out excess water, remove shiitake stems, and thinly slice both types of mushrooms. Shred the pork loin and cut tofu into thin strips. Prepare the cornstarch slurry by mixing cornstarch with water.

instructions.title: Cook Pork and Build Base
instructions.text: In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat 1 tablespoon of cooking oil over medium-high heat. Add the shredded pork and stir-fry until it turns white and is cooked through, about 2-3 minutes. Remove the pork from the pot and set aside.

instructions.title: Simmer Vegetables and Broth
instructions.text: Add the sliced shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, and julienned bamboo shoots to the pot. Stir-fry for 2 minutes. Pour in the chicken broth and bring it to a boil. Reduce heat to medium-low, add the cooked pork and tofu strips, then let it simmer for 10 minutes to allow flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Season the Soup
instructions.text: Stir in the soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper. Taste and adjust the seasoning, adding more vinegar or pepper if desired for your preferred level of hot and sourness. This is a crucial step for balancing the flavors.

instructions.title: Thicken and Add Egg
instructions.text: Slowly pour the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup while continuously stirring to prevent lumps. The soup should thicken to your desired consistency. Once thickened, slowly drizzle the beaten egg into the soup in a thin stream while stirring gently with chopsticks or a fork to create delicate egg ribbons.

instructions.title: Finish and Serve
instructions.text: Remove the soup from heat. Stir in the sesame oil. Ladle the hot and sour soup into individual bowls. Garnish generously with chopped scallions. For an extra kick, a drizzle of chili oil can be added to each serving. Serve immediately and enjoy the authentic flavors.

tips: Adjust the balance of rice vinegar and white pepper to your personal preference; some like it tangier, others spicier. Taste as you go!
For vegetarian Hot and Sour Soup, omit the pork and use vegetable broth. You can also add other vegetables like carrots or water chestnuts.
Serve the soup immediately for the best texture and flavor. Leftovers can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days and reheated gently on the stovetop.
article:
The tantalizing aroma of a perfectly balanced Hot and Sour Soup is enough to awaken anyone's appetite. A cornerstone of Chinese cuisine, this beloved dish delivers a symphony of flavors that dance on the palate – a sharp tang, a gentle heat, and a savory depth, all encapsulated in a silky, comforting broth. While often enjoyed as an appetizer in restaurants, mastering a homemade Hot and Sour Soup recipe allows you to experience its authentic complexity and warmth right in your own kitchen. Forget the sometimes-greasy or overly sweet versions; this guide will help you create a truly satisfying bowl that rivals your favorite takeout.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

Hot and Sour Soup, or *Suan La Tang* in Mandarin, is a quintessential Chinese soup renowned for its distinctive flavor profile. Originating from Sichuan province, it has since become a staple across various regional Chinese cuisines and is a ubiquitous offering in Chinese restaurants worldwide. The "hot" element typically comes from white pepper and, optionally, a dash of chili oil, providing a subtle warmth rather than an overpowering burn. The "sour" is primarily derived from rice vinegar, offering a bright, acidic counterpoint. Beyond these two defining characteristics, the soup boasts a rich umami base, often from chicken or vegetable broth, soy sauce, and rehydrated dried mushrooms.

The texture is another hallmark of Hot and Sour Soup. It's usually thickened to a pleasant consistency with cornstarch, allowing the various ingredients to suspend beautifully within the broth. Common additions include slivers of bamboo shoots for a delicate crunch, tender strips of wood ear mushrooms, soft cubes of tofu, and delicate ribbons of egg, all contributing to a delightful textural contrast with every spoonful. It’s a soup that warms you from the inside out, stimulates your taste buds, and offers a comforting embrace.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting an authentic Hot and Sour Soup recipe requires a few key ingredients, many of which are pantry staples in Asian cooking. Quality ingredients make a significant difference in the final flavor.

For the Broth Base:
*   6 cups chicken or vegetable broth (low sodium preferred)
*   1 tablespoon soy sauce (or tamari for gluten-free)
*   1 teaspoon dark soy sauce (for color, optional)

For the "Hot" and "Sour" Elements:
*   1/4 cup rice vinegar (unseasoned)
*   1-2 tablespoons white pepper (adjust to your spice preference)
*   1 teaspoon sesame oil
*   Chili oil to taste (optional, for extra heat)

The Solids:
*   1 oz dried shiitake mushrooms (about 4-5 medium caps)
*   1 oz dried wood ear mushrooms
*   4 oz firm or extra-firm tofu, drained and cut into small matchsticks
*   1/2 cup canned bamboo shoots, drained and cut into small matchsticks
*   1 large egg, whisked

For Thickening:
*   3 tablespoons cornstarch
*   1/4 cup cold water

Garnish (Optional):
*   2 green onions, thinly sliced
*   Fresh cilantro, chopped

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Here's how to craft your perfect Hot and Sour Soup recipe from scratch, ensuring a flavorful and texturally rich experience.

1.  **Prepare the Mushrooms:** Place dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in separate bowls. Cover with hot water and let them rehydrate for at least 30 minutes, or until soft. Once rehydrated, drain, squeeze out excess water, remove the tough stems from shiitake mushrooms, and thinly slice both types of mushrooms.
2.  **Combine Broth and Seasonings:** In a large pot or Dutch oven, combine the chicken or vegetable broth, soy sauce, and dark soy sauce (if using). Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer over medium heat.
3.  **Add Solids:** Add the sliced rehydrated mushrooms, tofu matchsticks, and bamboo shoots to the simmering broth. Let it simmer gently for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to meld.
4.  **Introduce Heat and Sour:** Stir in the rice vinegar and white pepper. Taste and adjust the white pepper according to your desired level of spiciness. Remember, white pepper has a distinct, pungent heat.
5.  **Thicken the Soup:** In a small bowl, whisk together the cornstarch and cold water until a smooth slurry forms. Slowly pour the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup in a thin, steady stream, stirring constantly. Continue stirring until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. If you prefer it thicker, you can add a little more cornstarch slurry.
6.  **Add Egg Ribbons:** Once the soup has thickened, remove it from direct heat or lower the heat to its lowest setting. Slowly drizzle the whisked egg into the soup in a thin stream, stirring gently with a fork or chopsticks in a circular motion. This will create delicate egg ribbons. Do not stir too vigorously, or the egg will break up too much.
7.  **Finishing Touches:** Stir in the sesame oil. If desired, add a few drops of chili oil for an extra kick. Taste the soup and adjust any seasonings – you might want more vinegar for tang, more soy sauce for saltiness, or more white pepper for heat.
8.  **Serve:** Ladle the hot soup into bowls and garnish with fresh sliced green onions and cilantro, if using.

Following this Hot and Sour Soup recipe will yield a truly satisfying and authentic bowl of Chinese comfort.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

To elevate your Hot and Sour Soup recipe from good to absolutely fantastic, keep these pro tips in mind:

*   **Quality Broth is Key:** A flavorful, good-quality chicken or vegetable broth forms the backbone of your soup. If using store-bought, opt for low-sodium to control the overall saltiness.
*   **Don't Skimp on White Pepper:** White pepper is crucial for that characteristic "hot" element. Don't be shy; it provides a unique warmth that black pepper cannot replicate.
*   **Balance is Everything:** Hot and Sour Soup is all about balance. Taste as you go and adjust the vinegar, white pepper, and soy sauce. It should be distinctly hot and sour, but neither should overpower the other or the savory base.
*   **Perfecting the Thickener:** Always mix cornstarch with cold water before adding it to hot soup. This prevents lumps. Add it slowly, stirring constantly, until you reach your desired consistency. The soup will thicken a bit more as it cools.
*   **Gentle Egg Drizzle:** For beautiful, wispy egg ribbons, pour the whisked egg slowly into the soup while stirring gently in one direction. Don't over-stir once the egg is in, or it will break into tiny pieces.
*   **Rehydrate Mushrooms Properly:** Fully rehydrated mushrooms contribute significantly to the soup's umami depth and texture. Don't rush this step.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile. It makes a fantastic appetizer, whetting the palate for a larger meal. Serve it alongside other Chinese favorites like fried rice, lo mein, spring rolls, or General Tso's chicken for a complete feast. For a lighter meal, it can be a satisfying main course on its own. Garnish with a sprinkle of fresh green onions or cilantro for a burst of color and freshness. A small drizzle of extra chili oil at the table allows individual diners to customize their heat level.

## Variations and Substitutions

One of the great things about this Hot and Sour Soup recipe is its adaptability. Feel free to experiment with different ingredients to suit your taste or dietary needs.

*   **Proteins:** For a heartier soup, add thinly sliced pork loin, chicken breast, or even shrimp during step 3. Ensure any meat is cooked through before proceeding. For vegetarians, simply add more tofu or mushrooms.
*   **Vegetables:** While bamboo shoots and mushrooms are traditional, you can add other thinly sliced vegetables like carrots, snow peas, bok choy, or water chestnuts for extra crunch and nutrients.
*   **Spiciness:** If you prefer more heat, increase the white pepper or add a few drops of hot chili oil directly into the broth. Sriracha can also be used, though it will alter the authentic flavor slightly.
*   **Sourness:** Adjust the amount of rice vinegar to achieve your preferred level of tang. Some recipes include a touch of black vinegar for a deeper, more complex sour note.
*   **Gluten-Free:** Easily make this soup gluten-free by using tamari instead of regular soy sauce.
*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** Use vegetable broth, omit the egg, and ensure your soy sauce is vegan-friendly. Increase the amount of tofu and mushrooms for a satisfying texture.

Armed with this comprehensive Hot and Sour Soup recipe, you're ready to create a truly delicious and authentic Chinese culinary experience at home. Enjoy the rich, complex flavors and the comforting warmth of this incredible soup!
